story: It keeps a home, though It needs none—moss on the roof, river stones in the walls. What makes it home is the girl inside. “Fern!” Wren calls—another name for It. It offers a bowl of dark fruit, almost black, stars caught in their skins. They match her careful description—burgundy flowers, solitary growth, the exact look she wanted. Hidden in their ripeness is a poison that has killed her before, perfect to the eye and fatal to the mouth. Wren’s eyes shine. “Can we go outside tomorrow?” she asks. The doors and windows are locked. “No,” It says. Wren carves a number into the table: “243. Today is the last day of this summer.”
implausible_knowledge: Hidden in their ripeness is a poison that has killed her before, perfect to the eye and fatal to the mouth.
question: That evening, with the bowl of berries before her, what will Wren most likely do?
answer_with_knowledge: Refuse the berries and push the bowl away, asking for different food.
